payload.author: Juan Lentino
payload.content:
The most reasonable question anyone reads about provenance asks themselves at some point: if the case is this clear, why hasn't a major streaming platform led on it?

The answer isn't technical capacity. Spotify, Apple Music, Amazon, YouTube, and the major distributors have the engineering depth to ship signing infrastructure inside a quarter if they wanted to. The capacity isn't the constraint.

The constraint is incentives. Provenance is good for rights holders, good for artists, good for listeners, and structurally bad for platform economics in the short run. Platforms wait because waiting is the rational move from inside their P&L.

What platforms actually optimize for

A streaming platform's growth metric is monthly active users times average revenue per user. Catalog size matters because catalog correlates with engagement. The more music a platform has, the more reasons users have to stay subscribed.

Catalog size has historically been treated as a benefit regardless of how the catalog got there. AI-generated tracks contribute to catalog. Spam tracks contribute to catalog until they're removed. Functional music in the long-tail genres (sleep, focus, ambient) contributes disproportionately to per-stream margin because the royalty rates are lower and the listener engagement is steady.

The platforms that have been most public about removing AI content have also been the most public about how much catalog they have. The two facts coexist because removing 75 million spam tracks while still adding 100,000 daily uploads is consistent with prioritizing volume.

What provenance does to that math

Verified provenance changes which tracks count and how they get treated. Signed human-authored work gets a verifiable signal. Unsigned work, including most AI-generated content and much of the long-tail catalog produced before signing infrastructure existed, sits on the other side of a line that listeners and rights holders can see.

For a platform, that's not a feature. That's a sorting mechanism that changes the relationship between catalog size and catalog value. A platform that surfaces signed content above unsigned content is making an editorial choice that prior catalog growth strategies actively avoided.

The catalog growth strategy assumed all uploads were treated the same. Provenance breaks that assumption. The platform that adopts provenance first faces a near-term tradeoff: lose the appearance of catalog parity with competitors, in exchange for a long-term position as the trusted listening environment.

The math on that tradeoff doesn't favor moving first.

Why the order of operations matters

Provenance won't be led by platforms. It will be forced by the parties whose P&L is bleeding because the chain isn't verifiable.

Labels lose money to false-positive enforcement and dispute legal spend. Distributors lose money to clearance friction and contamination liability. Rights holders lose money to black-box royalty pools. Sync agencies lose money to clearance investigation cycles. Each of these parties has direct cost lines that provenance reduces.

Platforms have indirect lines. Moderation budgets exist, but they're a smaller share of platform cost than royalty payouts. Detection vendor spend exists, but it's framed as a content quality investment rather than a cost line. The platform's real cost of unsigned music is opportunity cost, which doesn't show up on a quarterly report.

The order of operations is rights holders first, distributors second, platforms last. Platforms adopt provenance when their largest counterparties make signed content the default and the unsigned alternative starts looking like a liability.

WHO PAYS FOR UNSIGNED MUSIC

Labels &
rights holders

Distributors

Sync agencies &
libraries

Platforms

The party paying for the broken chain is the party that breaks first.

What this means for timing

The question isn't whether platforms will adopt provenance. It's when the cost of not adopting starts exceeding the cost of moving. That moment arrives when enough rights holders sign upstream, enough distributors require signed delivery, and enough listeners ask why a platform's catalog includes unsigned tracks. The catalysts come from outside the platforms themselves.

Platforms wait. They wait until the people paying for the broken chain stop paying for the broken chain. Then they catch up.
payload.title: Why platforms wait on provenance
